title: Sum of Two Numbers
description: The sum of digits of a non-negative integer $$$a$$$ is the result of summing up its digits together when written in the decimal system. For example, the sum of digits of $$$123$$$ is $$$6$$$ and the sum of digits of $$$10$$$ is $$$1$$$. In a formal way, the sum of digits of $$$\displaystyle a=\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} a_i \cdot 10^i$$$, where $$$0 \leq a_i \leq 9$$$, is defined as $$$\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{\infty}{a_i}$$$. Given an integer $$$n$$$, find two non-negative integers $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$ which satisfy the following conditions. $$$x+y=n$$$, and the sum of digits of $$$x$$$ and the sum of digits of $$$y$$$ differ by at most $$$1$$$. It can be shown that such $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$ always exist.
input_spec: Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10\,000$$$). Each test case consists of a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1 \leq n \leq 10^9$$$)
output_spec: For each test case, print two integers $$$x$$$ and $$$y$$$. If there are multiple answers, print any.

routes.name: Digit-by-digit greedy split
routes.description: Process n one digit at a time from the most significant digit to the least. Split each digit as evenly as possible between x and y, alternating which number gets the extra 1 when the digit is odd, so the digit sums stay within 1 of each other.

observations.title: Digit sum is additive over individual digits
observations.description: The digit sum of a number equals the sum of its individual digits. If we construct x and y digit by digit without carries, then digitSum(x) = sum of x's digits and digitSum(y) = sum of y's digits. This means we can control the digit sums by controlling how each digit of n is split between x and y.

hints.nudge: What property of digit sums lets you reason about them one digit at a time?
hints.question: If you build x and y by choosing their digits independently at each position, how does digitSum(x) relate to the individual digit choices?
hints.formal: digitSum(x) = sum of x_i for each digit position i. If we construct x and y so that x_i + y_i = n_i (the i-th digit of n) with no carries, then digitSum(x) + digitSum(y) = digitSum(n), and we fully control the distribution.

observations.title: Avoid carries by splitting each digit independently
observations.description: If for each digit position we choose x_i and y_i such that x_i + y_i equals the corresponding digit of n (with 0 <= x_i, y_i <= 9), then no carrying occurs and x + y = n is guaranteed. This reduces the problem to a per-digit splitting task.

hints.nudge: What happens if x_i + y_i exceeds 9 at some position? How can you avoid that complication?
hints.question: If the digit of n at position i is d, and you pick x_i = a and y_i = d - a where 0 <= a <= d, does any carry propagate? Why does this simplify the problem?
hints.formal: For each digit d of n, set x_i = floor(d/2) and y_i = ceil(d/2) (or vice versa). Since d <= 9, both x_i and y_i are between 0 and 9, so no carry occurs and x + y = n.

observations.title: Split each digit as evenly as possible
observations.description: For a digit d, the most balanced split is floor(d/2) and ceil(d/2). When d is even, both parts contribute equally to their digit sums. When d is odd, one part gets 1 more than the other. The total digit-sum difference comes entirely from how many odd digits give their larger half to x versus y.

hints.nudge: For a single digit d, what split of d into two parts minimizes the difference in the parts?
hints.question: If d = 7, the split is 3 and 4. One side gets 1 more digit-sum than the other. When does this matter, and when is it free?
hints.formal: For even d: x_i = y_i = d/2, contributing 0 to the digit-sum difference. For odd d: one gets floor(d/2) and the other ceil(d/2), contributing +1 or -1 to the running difference.

observations.title: Alternate the surplus on odd digits
observations.description: Each odd digit creates a surplus of 1 that must go to either x or y. To keep the total digit-sum difference within 1, alternate which number receives the larger half each time an odd digit is encountered. This ensures the running difference never exceeds 1 in absolute value.

hints.nudge: If several digits of n are odd, how do you decide which number gets the bigger share each time?
hints.question: Imagine a running counter tracking digitSum(x) - digitSum(y). Each odd digit moves it by +1 or -1. How do you keep it in [-1, 1]?
hints.formal: Maintain a flag or counter. For each odd digit d: if the counter is 0 or negative, give ceil(d/2) to x (counter += 1); otherwise give ceil(d/2) to y (counter -= 1). The counter stays in {-1, 0, 1} throughout.

observations.title: Build x and y as numbers from their digit arrays
observations.description: After determining each digit of x and y, reconstruct the actual integers. Process the digits of n from the most significant to the least significant, appending each chosen digit to x and y. The result is two integers that sum to n with digit sums differing by at most 1.

hints.nudge: Once you know each digit of x and y, how do you turn those digit sequences into integers?
hints.question: If you process n as a string and build x_digits and y_digits arrays, how do you convert them to numbers for output?
hints.formal: Convert n to a string. Iterate over each character (digit d). Compute a = floor(d/2), b = d - a. If d is odd, swap a and b based on the alternation flag. Append a to x_str, b to y_str. Parse both strings to integers for output.
